ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for cheese prata; mapped to the nearest parent class 'flatbread food product' as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Flat bread) is the closest defensible category for prata/roti canai-style fried flatbread; no cheese-filled variant code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for cheese prata or roti prata as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of cheese prata is approximately 180 g, equivalent to one piece of prata filled with cheese, typically served with a small cup of curry dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Prata, cheese — Health Promotion Board Singapore Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database at confidence 0.95 with exact dish match for cheese prata. Saturated fat (7.5 g/100g) and trans fat (0.25 g/100g) were originally tagged to a delivery platform in the input record at confidence 0.70; these values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for 'Bread, pita, white, enriched' (FDC ID 325871) and 'Cheese, cheddar' (FDC ID 328637) as component analogues and are retained as plausible — saturated fat of 7.5 g/100g is consistent with a cheese-and-ghee-enriched flatbread. Vitamin C of 0.0 mg/100g is confirmed zero for a flour-and-cheese product with no vegetable or fruit components, consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for analogous products.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on roti/chapati category entries in the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cheese prata in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 57 is estimated from the closest analogous food: plain roti/chapati (GI range 52–62, medium category) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and reported in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. The addition of cheese and ghee slightly lowers the effective GI by increasing fat content, which slows gastric emptying; a midpoint estimate of 57 is used. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (57) × available carbohydrate per serving (75.23 g/100g × 180 g / 100) ÷ 100 = 57 × 43.0 / 100 ≈ 24.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 632 mg/100g exceeds the 600 mg/100g hypertension threshold; saturated fat 7.5 g/100g from cheese and ghee contributes to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit cheese prata due to sodium at 632 mg/100g exceeding safe thresholds; if consumed, request reduced cheese and no added salt, avoid processed-meat variants, and use curry dipping sauce sparingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Total carbohydrate is high at 75.2 g/100g; however, dietary fibre at 10.2 g/100g moderates glycaemic response, and sugar is low at 2.9 g/100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes may consume cheese prata in a controlled portion of one piece (approximately 180 g) paired with a protein-rich curry to slow gastric emptying, while monitoring total carbohydrate intake and avoiding banana or sweetened variants.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 7.5 g/100g from cheese and ghee, trans fat at 0.25 g/100g, and sodium at 632 mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit cheese prata due to high saturated fat (7.5 g/100g) and trans fat (0.25 g/100g) from ghee and cheese combined with elevated sodium; if consumed occasionally, request less cheese, substitute ghee with vegetable oil, and avoid processed-meat variants.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.5 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; sodium 632 mg/100g worsens tissue inflammation and fluid retention.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid cheese prata due to pro-inflammatory saturated fat from cheese and ghee exceeding 5 g/100g and sodium exceeding RA-safe thresholds; if consumed, request lower-fat cheese, substitute ghee with cooking oil, and omit processed-meat fillings.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese prata is a popular Singaporean Indian fried flatbread made from a laminated dough of plain flour, water, ghee, egg, and salt, filled with melted cheese — typically cheddar or processed cheese slices — and cooked on a flat iron griddle until golden and crispy on the outside with a soft, flaky, layered interior. It is a variant of roti prata (also known as roti canai in Malaysia), a South Indian-origin flatbread that has become a staple of Singapore's hawker culture, commonly served at Indian Muslim (mamak) stalls for breakfast, lunch, or supper. The cheese filling adds a rich, savoury, slightly salty flavour and a creamy melt to the characteristic buttery, flaky texture of the prata. It is typically served with a small cup of fish or mutton curry for dipping. Nutritionally, cheese prata is energy-dense at approximately 412 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein content of 9.85 g/100g from flour and cheese, a relatively low total fat of 8.25 g/100g but a high saturated fat fraction of 7.5 g/100g from ghee and cheese, and a high carbohydrate content of 75.2 g/100g. Dietary fibre is notably high at 10.2 g/100g, which moderates the glycaemic response. Sodium is elevated at 632 mg/100g, making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis without modification. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 57 (medium), consistent with other flatbreads. Cheese prata is halal and vegetarian when prepared without meat fillings. Common variants include egg prata, banana prata, mushroom prata, and hotdog cheese prata.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database at confidence 0.95 with exact dish match for cheese prata. Saturated fat and trans fat values cross-validated as plausible against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component analogues and retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using roti/chapati as the closest approved-source equivalent, clearly documented with peer-reviewed citation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cheese prata is not available in the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB entry for cheese prata and are not available in an approved source for this specific dish as of 2026. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB Singapore; confirmed zero per the HPB source. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 confirmed zero — cheese prata contains no vegetable or fruit components that would contribute vitamin C, consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for analogous flour-and-cheese products. trans_fat_g of 0.25 g/100g is retained from the input record and cross-validated as plausible given the use of ghee and commercial margarine in some prata preparations, consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for similar fried flatbreads. The input record listed three portions each with is_default true, which is inconsistent; the 180 g portion (prata with egg and cheese) has been selected as the default serving size for GI and GL calculations as it most closely matches a standard single-piece serving. The input cultural tags listed 'vegetarian' which is accurate for the plain cheese variant but not for hotdog cheese prata; the dietary flag is set to true for the canonical cheese-only preparation. Primary cuisine corrected from 'Malay' (input) to 'Singaporean Indian' reflecting the South Indian Tamil Muslim origin of roti prata in Singapore's hawker culture. Nutrient source updated to HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database (hpb.gov.sg) as the approved primary source; delivery platform data from the input record was not used as a primary evidence source.
